channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Pulled in at Least $2.2 Billion in 2025, Financial Disclosure Shows
item.description: The release of a mandatory financial disclosure for 2025 shows that the Trump family’s holdings, particularly the president’s crypto businesses, were stunningly lucrative.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump entering the White House on Sunday. His mandatory financial disclosure report for 2025 pulled back the curtain on his secretive business operations.

item.title: Supreme Court Blocks Trump’s Push to End Birthright Citizenship
item.description: Also, a House Republican says depression led to his long absence. Here's the latest at the end of Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court chamber in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Supreme Court Lifts Spending Limits on Political Parties and Candidates
item.description: Republicans had asked the court to strike down restrictions on how much political parties can spend in coordination with candidates.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Adam Liptak
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delegates and supporters at the Republican National Convention in 2024. The National Republican Senatorial Committee brought a legal challenge against limits on coordinated spending when JD Vance ran for Senate in 2022.

item.title: Tom Kean, New Jersey Congressman, Says Depression Led to Long Absence
item.description: After months of mystery, the New Jersey representative broke his silence about the undisclosed health issue that prompted his 117-day absence.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas H. Kean Jr., a New Jersey Republican, at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: What Tom Kean Did and Didn’t Explain About His Absence
item.description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr., a New Jersey Republican, has finally reappeared in Congress. Questions remain about his nearly four-month absence from public life, which he said was due to depression.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr., Republican of New Jersey, left many questions unanswered in his speech to the House of Representatives on Tuesday.

item.title: For Many Americans, Depression Is Familiar. Here’s What We Know.
item.description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. announced he had been hospitalized for depression. More than one in four U.S. adults report having been diagnosed with the condition.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Goldstein and Dana G. Smith
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. appeared on Capitol Hill on Tuesday following a lengthy absence.

item.title: The Two Mexico Cities Fighting for Claim Over Soccer’s Origin
item.description: Mineral del Monte, a small former mining town that became a tourist destination partly because of its Cornish influence, claims that soccer in Mexico started there. Well, so does the nearby city of Pachuca.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tourists wearing Mexican national team jerseys in the center of Mineral del Monte, Mexico on Wednesday.

item.title: Talarico Seizes on Paxton Tabloid Story to Test Corruption Attacks in Texas Senate Race
item.description: After a salacious report about Ken Paxton, the Republican nominee for Senate in Texas, his Democratic rival, James Talarico, seized on the news — but focused on corruption and affordability.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times; Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton and James Talarico are locked in a competitive race for Senate in Texas.

item.title: Trump Announces Republican Midterm Convention in Dallas, Texas
item.description: Parties normally hold conventions only in presidential years, but President Trump liked the idea of mobilizing Republicans this fall.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dallas will be the site of Republican efforts to mobilize ahead of the Nov. 3 midterm elections.

item.title: Texas Is a Tossup. The Times/Siena Poll Points to How It Got There.
item.description: A major shift among Hispanic voters and a favorable candidate matchup have helped put Democrats on the doorstep of a Senate upset.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico received positive ratings on every question asked in the survey.

item.title: 4 Warning Signs for Graham Platner. And 4 for Susan Collins Too.
item.description: The Maine race, which both parties see as key to winning control of the Senate, is extremely close, a Times/Portland Press Herald/Siena poll found. Both candidates have reasons for concern.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times; Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner and Susan Collins

item.title: Stocks Notch Strongest Quarter Since 2020
item.description: The S&P 500 rose almost 15 percent for the three months through June, and many stock analysts remain optimistic that corporate earnings driven by artificial intelligence will keep growing.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Record-Breaking Heat Wave Expected to Hit New York City Ahead of July 4 Weekend
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani signed an executive order that aims to protect people who work outdoors. Temperatures this week could exceed 100 degrees.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Goldstein and Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thursday and Friday are expected to bring three-digit temperatures to New York City, which hasn’t had back-to-back 100-degree since 2011.

item.title: G.O.P. Revolt Paralyzes the House, Stalling Trump Agenda
item.description: A Republican blockade derailed a Pentagon policy bill and other legislation as the far right pressed for action on a voting bill championed by President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison, Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: States Sue Trump Administration Over Medicaid Work Requirements
item.description: The lawsuit argues that new federal rules went beyond what Congress enacted and broke from guidance that the federal government previewed to states.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration is being sued on the new Medicaid work requirements, arguing that the guidelines are inconsistent with federal law.

item.title: C.I.A. Reorganization Prioritizes Cyberoperations
item.description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, said that the agency would take “smart risks,” but that people would have oversight of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, said the overhaul was an acknowledgment that in the modern world, digital borders are as important as physical borders.

item.title: Court Halts Pentagon Rule Requiring Escorts for Journalists
item.description: A federal judge temporarily blocked a new rule that restricted reporters’ access to the Pentagon, in a lawsuit brought by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times challenged the Pentagon’s rule as an unconstitutional infringement of the First Amendment.

item.title: After a Tap Heard in Rubble, U.S. Rescue Teams Begin a Grueling Marathon
item.description: Surrounded by the devastation of Venezuela’s earthquakes, emergency specialists from California, Virginia and Florida work with locals to search for survivors.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler and Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Matias Delacroix/Associated Press
item.media:description: Members of the Fairfax County Urban Search and Rescue team from Virginia searched for people in the rubble of a collapsed building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Sunday.

item.title: Trump Officials Sideline Machado, Venezuela’s Opposition Leader, Over Earthquake Response
item.description: U.S. officials called a bid by María Corina Machado, the Nobel Peace Prize winner, to return to earthquake-battered Venezuela a “political stunt” that has distracted from recovery efforts.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of civilian volunteers search for earthquake survivors in Caribe, Venezuela, on Saturday.

item.title: Aid Workers in Venezuela Fear Disease Outbreaks After Twin Quakes
item.description: Health care workers worry that illnesses like cholera could spread in areas where clean water was already in short supply, and last week’s earthquakes destroyed water systems.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: International Committee of the Red Cross
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: Collapsed buildings in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Monday. The death toll is above 1,900.

item.title: The Prom Went On in Kyiv, but Masha’s Date Danced Alone
item.description: Masha Polska, 15, was an avid dancer who had been dreaming of a star turn in the group waltz. That was not to be.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady, Liubov Sholudko and Oksana Parafeniuk
item.media:description: Yehor Holodryha, center, and classmates of Masha Polska dancing the waltz at their prom in Kyiv, Ukraine, in June.

item.title: Rays of Hope in Dark Times
item.description: It’s time to update Learned Hand’s great “Spirit of Liberty” address.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens

item.title: The Supreme Court Gets Birthright Citizenship Right
item.description: The Constitution is clear. People who are born in this country and subject to its laws are citizens.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: Curiosity, Perseverance and Now Promise? NASA May Send a Mars Rover to the Moon.
item.description: As part of plans to establish a lunar base, the space agency may send to the moon a spare test version of the rover — as well as a soccer ball.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Astrobotic/Intuitive Machines/Firefly
item.media:description: NASA announced it had awarded additional contracts for robotic landers to companies the agency had previously hired.
